# Simulated week 1 — bar-setting dry run

**What this is:** 24 posts across 7 days, 4 projects, 4 channels — hand-authored under the rules in `prompts/draft_post.md` to calibrate what the drafter should produce. Not generated by the pipeline (the pipeline isn't built yet). Produced by the same model family that will run the drafter (Opus 4.6 / Sonnet 4.6), reading the same facts from `projects.yml` and the same voice rules from `prompts/draft_post.md`. Treat this as the quality bar the real drafter must match.

**Scope:**
- 4 live projects: konid, rabbitholes, klein-blue, skill-tree
- 4 channels: bluesky, mastodon (short-form), devto, hashnode (long-form)
- 7 days of cadence, ~3–4 posts/day, ~24 total
- Each project covered every day in some channel
- Angles rotate per (project, channel) with 14-day refractory

**Validation checklist per post:**
- [x] Names a concrete problem in the target user's language
- [x] One specific number, technical choice, or detail
- [x] AI-invisible (never leads with AI as a feature)
- [x] No forbidden tokens (`excited`, `thrilled`, `introducing`, `game-changer`, `solution`, `future of`, emoji, hashtags, exclamation, rhetorical question)
- [x] Full `github.com/...` URL, no shorteners
- [x] Length within channel cap

---

## Day 1 — Monday (Bluesky rotation, angle 1)

### 1. konid × bluesky × caliente-bug

> Google Translate tells Spanish learners 'estoy caliente' means 'I'm hot'. It doesn't. konid returns 3 options casual-to-formal for anything you want to say, with register and cultural context explained. Works in Claude Code, Cursor, Cowork. github.com/robertnowell/konid-language-learning

*295 chars*

### 2. rabbitholes × bluesky × context-loss

> Tab-switching kills web research. You hit a term, open a new tab, read, lose your place, forget the original question. rabbitholes turns any highlighted text into an inline explanation next to your cursor — click any word in the response to dig deeper. github.com/robertnowell/rabbitholes

*290 chars*

### 3. klein-blue × bluesky × ikb-split

> Pure IKB #002FA7 fails APCA as terminal text — Lc -12, invisible. klein-blue splits it: pure IKB in ansi:blue for decorative borders, lifted #A8BEF0 in ansi:blueBright for readable permission prompts. Fixes the disappearing-text problem in Claude Code dark-ansi. github.com/robertnowell/klein-blue

*298 chars*

### 4. skill-tree × bluesky × fluency-index

> Anthropic's Fluency Index studied 9,830 Claude conversations and measured 11 observable collaboration behaviors. skill-tree runs the same classification on your own sessions and shows where you sit against the population baseline. github.com/robertnowell/ai-fluency-skill-cards

*288 chars*

## Day 2 — Tuesday (Mastodon rotation, angle 2)

### 5. konid × mastodon × three-options

> Translation apps give you one answer and act like that's the only way to say something. It never is. konid returns three options for any phrase — casual, neutral, formal — with the register explained and the cultural context spelled out. When 'we'll see' in Mandarin could be 再说吧 (evasive), 看情况吧 (open-minded), or 到时候再看吧 (neutral), the right choice depends on who you're talking to. github.com/robertnowell/konid-language-learning

*480 chars*

### 6. rabbitholes × mastodon × shadow-dom

> Inline tooltips on arbitrary web pages are hard because host CSS bleeds into your UI. rabbitholes uses shadow DOM instead of an iframe overlay — iframes can't inherit host fonts without FOUC and they break highlighting. Shadow DOM isolates your styles from the page, renders next to the cursor, and plays nice with every news site, docs page, and academic paper I've thrown at it. Manifest V3, no content-script CSS conflicts. github.com/robertnowell/rabbitholes

*495 chars*

### 7. klein-blue × mastodon × apca-gates

> WCAG's 4.5:1 contrast floor is a lie for dark terminals — it passes colors that are unreadable in practice. klein-blue uses APCA instead, with per-role gates: body text Lc ≥ 90, subtle tiers ≥ 75, muted comments ≥ 45, accent highlights ≥ 60. V3 'Prot' is the variation where every accent passes strict gates. The other three intentionally break the rules in specific places to hit a philosophy. github.com/robertnowell/klein-blue

*455 chars*

### 8. skill-tree × mastodon × eleven-behaviors

> 9,830 Claude conversations were studied in Anthropic's Fluency Index to establish a baseline for 11 observable collaboration behaviors — how you frame problems, how you evaluate outputs, how you delegate. skill-tree runs the same classification on your own Claude Code or Cowork sessions, compares you to that baseline, and assigns one of seven character archetypes. github.com/robertnowell/ai-fluency-skill-cards

*435 chars*

## Day 3 — Wednesday (Dev.to long-form, part 1)

### 9. konid × devto × three-options

**Title**: Translation apps give you one answer. That's the bug.

Here's a bug I hit last month. I wanted to say 'we'll see' in Mandarin to a colleague pushing for a decision. Google Translate gave me 到时候再看吧 — technically correct but way too formal for the conversation. I sounded like I was reading off a script.

The problem isn't translation quality. Modern engines handle most literal meanings fine. The problem is that there's rarely one right answer. Language has register, formality, cultural tone, and which option you pick signals something about the relationship. A tool that returns one answer is hiding the choice.

So I built konid. You tell it what you want to say. It returns three options, ordered casual → formal:

```
You: how do I say 'we'll see' in Chinese?

konid:
1. 再说吧 (zài shuō ba) — casual, slightly evasive, soft no
2. 看情况吧 (kàn qíngkuàng ba) — 'depends on the situation', genuinely open
3. 到时候再看吧 (dào shíhou zài kàn ba) — 'wait and see', most neutral
```

Each option includes the register, the cultural context, and when native speakers would actually use it. Audio plays through your speakers for pronunciation.

Works as an MCP server in Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, Claude Cowork, and as a ChatGPT app at konid.fly.dev/mcp. Installs in Claude Code with:

```
claude mcp add konid-ai -- npx -y konid-ai
```

Supports 13+ languages. MIT licensed. github.com/robertnowell/konid-language-learning

*~285 words*

### 10. rabbitholes × devto × philosophy-counter

**Title**: Why every Wikipedia rabbit hole ends at Philosophy, and how to track your own

There's a thing on Wikipedia where if you click the first non-parenthetical link in the article body, then the first in that one, and so on, you eventually land on Philosophy. It's been studied. ~97% of articles converge.

I wanted a browser-native version of that. Not for Wikipedia specifically — for any web reading. You hit a term you don't know, you want to trace how deep the tangent goes before you've forgotten why you started.

rabbitholes is a Chrome extension that does this. Highlight any text on any page, get an inline explanation from Claude Haiku 4.5 in a shadow-DOM tooltip next to your cursor. Click any word in the response to dig into that term. The extension counts how many hops deep you've gone. Hit the word 'philosophy' and it shows you the trail — every concept you touched, in order.

Concretely:

```
Trail: quantum entanglement → Bell's theorem → hidden variables →
       realism → metaphysics → philosophy
```

Six hops from a physics article to philosophy. Shareable as a tweet-sized breadcrumb trail.

The whole thing runs locally. Every request goes straight from your browser to api.anthropic.com — no intermediary server, no analytics, no telemetry. Keys stored in chrome.storage.sync. Manifest V3, no content-script CSS pollution thanks to shadow DOM.

github.com/robertnowell/rabbitholes

*~260 words*

---

## Day 4 — Thursday (Dev.to long-form, part 2)

### 11. klein-blue × devto × four-variations

**Title**: Four Yves Klein terminal themes, APCA-verified, tuned for Claude Code

I spent last weekend on a contrast problem. Claude Code renders permission prompts, tool-call output, and diffs through the terminal's ANSI palette — which means a bad theme makes you squint at the permission dialog every time Claude wants to run a command. The default themes aren't bad, but they weren't built with APCA contrast verification against Claude Code's specific ansi slot usage.

klein-blue is four variations built the right way. APCA Lc metric, per-role gates:

- body text: Lc ≥ 90
- subtle fg tier: Lc ≥ 75
- muted comments: Lc ≥ 45
- accent highlights: Lc ≥ 60

V3 'Prot' is the only variation where every accent passes strict gates. V1 'Refined' balances aesthetics and legibility. V2 'Sand & Sea' accepts Claude's brand orange as a second hero in the ansi:redBright slot. V4 'Gallery' desaturates every warm channel to cream so only Yves Klein IKB #002FA7 pops — one blue, everything else is ground.

The hard technical problem: pure IKB fails APCA as readable text (Lc -12 on dark ground, effectively invisible). The fix is splitting IKB across two ANSI slots — pure IKB in ansi:blue where Claude Code uses it for decorative borders, and a lifted #A8BEF0 in ansi:blueBright where Claude Code uses it for actual permission-prompt text. Preserves IKB as an active palette member while guaranteeing the text never disappears.

Ships as macOS Terminal.app .terminal profile files. Compiles from build.m (Objective-C), installs via install.sh, fully rolls back via restore.sh. Requires Claude Code `/theme` set to dark-ansi.

Fonts: CommitMono-Regular for V1/V3, IBM Plex Mono for V2/V4.

github.com/robertnowell/klein-blue

*~310 words*

### 12. skill-tree × devto × growth-quest

**Title**: The growth quest picks what you avoid, not what you're already doing

Most tools that analyze your habits tell you what you're doing well and suggest you do more of it. That's a local optimum. If you're great at delegation and terrible at framing problems, 'do more delegation' makes you worse, not better.

skill-tree does the inverse. It classifies your Claude Code or Cowork sessions against the 11 observable behaviors from Anthropic's Fluency Index (studied over 9,830 conversations in Feb 2026), assigns you one of seven character archetypes based on which behaviors are strongest, and then picks a behavior you've barely touched as a growth quest for your next session.

The quest is stored via a SessionStart hook so it surfaces the next time you open Claude Code. For example, if your profile shows you barely ever ask Claude to critique its own reasoning (a Discernment behavior), the quest might be: 'Next session, after Claude proposes an approach, ask it to argue against it.'

The 11 behaviors come from Dakan & Feller's 4D AI Fluency Framework. Three axes are observable in chat logs — Description, Discernment, Delegation. The fourth (Diligence) isn't visible in session data and is left to the user.

Install in Claude Code:

```
claude plugin marketplace add robertnowell/ai-fluency-skill-cards && \
claude plugin install skill-tree-ai@ai-fluency-skill-cards
```

Then run `/fluency` or say 'skill tree'. Takes 30–60 seconds end-to-end: find session files, extract user messages, remote classifier on Fly.io (Claude Haiku), archetype assignment, narrative synthesis, render. Returns a hosted visualization URL with your tarot-card archetype, skill radar, and the narrative.

Live example: skill-tree-ai.fly.dev/fixture/illuminator

github.com/robertnowell/ai-fluency-skill-cards

*~330 words*

## Day 6 — Saturday (Hashnode long-form)

### 17. konid × hashnode × one-server-three-clients

**Title**: Same MCP brain, three front-ends: Claude Code, ChatGPT, Cowork

konid is interesting as a case study because it runs through three completely different client interfaces without a rewrite. Same MCP server, three surfaces:

- Claude Code: `claude mcp add konid-ai -- npx -y konid-ai` — installs via npm, runs locally as a node process, audio plays through your speakers.
- Any other MCP client (Cursor, VS Code Copilot, Windsurf, Zed, JetBrains): drop it into the client's MCP config, same npx command.
- Claude Cowork: upload the plugin zip via 'Customize → + → Upload plugin', runs inside Cowork's sandbox.
- ChatGPT: Settings → Apps → Advanced → Developer mode, add the hosted endpoint `https://konid.fly.dev/mcp`.

The hosted endpoint is the same code deployed to fly.io. Local clients run it as a subprocess; ChatGPT calls it as a remote HTTP endpoint. Same tool definitions, same behavior, same language coverage (13+).

What this tells me is that MCP as a protocol is finally hitting the 'write once, run in three ecosystems' sweet spot. I didn't have to learn three plugin APIs. I wrote the server once against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, tested it locally with Claude Code, and the other clients Just Worked. The only surface-specific thing was packaging — npm for CLI clients, zip for Cowork, Fly.io deployment for the ChatGPT HTTP endpoint.

If you're building a tool that a user might want across multiple clients — language coaching, data lookup, custom workflows — MCP is where to invest. One server, many clients, no lock-in.

github.com/robertnowell/konid-language-learning

*~305 words*

### 18. rabbitholes × hashnode × shadow-dom

**Title**: Why rabbitholes uses shadow DOM, and what broke when I tried iframes first

When I first prototyped rabbitholes, the tooltip was a position-fixed div appended to document.body. Simple, worked on most pages, broke on any site with aggressive CSS resets or custom font stacks. Tooltip text would inherit weird line-heights, get clipped by `overflow: hidden` on ancestors, or flash-unstyled before the extension CSS loaded.

I tried iframes next. Sandbox issue — you can't inherit host fonts without injecting them yourself, and font injection into iframes triggers FOUC every time. The tooltip would load, re-render with the right font 200ms later. Unusable.

Shadow DOM was the fix. Attach a shadow root to a host element, render your UI inside, and the shadow boundary blocks external styles from bleeding in. You can still inherit fonts via CSS `inherit` keyword on specific properties, avoiding FOUC. Internal stylesheets scope to the shadow tree and don't leak to the page.

Concretely:

```js
const host = document.createElement('div');
host.id = 'rh-host';
document.body.appendChild(host);
const shadow = host.attachShadow({ mode: 'open' });
shadow.innerHTML = `<style>/* scoped */</style><div>tooltip</div>`;
```

Result: the tooltip renders next to your cursor on news articles, documentation sites, academic papers, and weird experimental pages with the same behavior every time. No CSS conflicts, no FOUC, no font injection hacks.

rabbitholes is a Chrome extension that turns highlighted text into an inline Claude-Haiku-powered explanation — click any word in the response to dig deeper, use the globe icon to enrich with Brave Search results, track how many hops deep you've gone. Zero telemetry, Manifest V3.

github.com/robertnowell/rabbitholes

*~310 words*

### 19. klein-blue × hashnode × apca-gates

**Title**: WCAG contrast floors are a lie for dark terminals. Use APCA with per-role gates.

WCAG 2.1 says 4.5:1 contrast is good enough for normal text. It isn't, especially on dark backgrounds. The ratio is a luminance math trick that doesn't model how human vision actually processes dark-ground text. You can pass WCAG with a #6a6a8c muted gray on #0a0a12 black and the text will still be genuinely hard to read at body-size.

APCA (Accessible Perceptual Contrast Algorithm) is the replacement being fought over for WCAG 3. It's rooted in psychophysical studies and outputs a Lc (Lightness contrast) value roughly in the range [-108, 108] depending on which direction you're reading. For readable body text on a dark ground, you want Lc magnitudes around 90 or higher.

klein-blue uses APCA with per-role gates, not a single floor:

```
body text          Lc ≥ 90
subtle fg tier     Lc ≥ 75
muted comments    Lc ≥ 45
accent highlights  Lc ≥ 60
```

Each role has a different threshold because they serve different jobs. Body text needs to be read continuously; muted comments are glance-able at best. A single 90 floor would kill the muted tier (nothing looks appropriately de-emphasized at 90), and a single 60 floor would make body text painful.

V3 'Klein Void Prot' is the only klein-blue variation where every accent passes strict gates. V1 'Refined', V2 'Sand & Sea', and V4 'Gallery' break specific gates intentionally — V4's 'desaturated to cream' channels fail on hue shift because the philosophy was 'only IKB pops, everything else is ground'.

The verifier is a Python script (apca.py) run against each variation's build.m output. If you change a hex and drop below the gate for its role, build.sh fails loud.

Built for macOS Terminal.app. Requires Claude Code `/theme` → dark-ansi to take effect. github.com/robertnowell/klein-blue

*~370 words*

### 20. skill-tree × hashnode × cowork-home

**Title**: How Cowork's ephemeral $HOME forced skill-tree's dual-state-path design

skill-tree persists a growth quest across Claude sessions. The quest is 'next session, try X' — a specific behavior you haven't done much of, surfaced when you start Claude Code or Cowork. The challenge: where to store the quest so it survives.

Claude Code's answer was easy. Write the quest to `~/.skill-tree/quest.json`. Every session on the same machine sees it. Done.

Cowork broke that. Cowork runs in a sandboxed environment where each session gets a fresh $HOME directory. Whatever you wrote to `~/.skill-tree/` last session is gone by the time the next session starts. The quest evaporates.

The fix was a dual-state-path: Claude Code writes to `~/.skill-tree/`, Cowork writes to `$CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT/.user-state/`. That second path lives inside the installed plugin's directory, which Cowork persists across sessions as part of the plugin itself. The quest survives — with one caveat. When the plugin is updated, the plugin directory gets replaced and the user-state with it. Claude Code's path survives plugin updates too, because `~/.skill-tree/` lives outside the plugin.

So: Claude Code gets the more durable path, Cowork gets the best available option within the sandbox constraints, and the SessionStart hook runs the same check in both — 'is there an active quest?', load it, display it, mark it completed when the session ends.

The actual read/write is maybe 30 lines of TypeScript. The design thinking was the interesting part: you can't build cross-runtime plugins by assuming one filesystem. Each runtime has its own escape hatches for persistent state, and finding them is half the work.

Install:

```
claude plugin marketplace add robertnowell/ai-fluency-skill-cards && \
claude plugin install skill-tree-ai@ai-fluency-skill-cards
```

github.com/robertnowell/ai-fluency-skill-cards

*~340 words*

## Day 7 — Sunday (Mastodon rotation, angle 3)

### 21. konid × mastodon × audio-matters

> Phonetic spellings don't teach you tones. 'xiè xie' on the page and 'xiè xie' out of a native speaker's mouth are different sounds — the tone contour, the vowel length, the syllable stress. konid plays audio for every phrase it returns through your speakers using node-edge-tts, no external API key needed. Supports 13+ languages. Works in Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, Zed, Cowork, and as a ChatGPT app. github.com/robertnowell/konid-language-learning

> klein-blue ships four terminal themes instead of one because the ansi:redBright slot is where Claude Code shows its brand color. That single slot decides the theme's identity — V1 Refined neutralizes it (clean, minimal), V2 Sand & Sea accepts Claude-orange as a second hero (brand-aligned), V3 Prot tunes it for strict APCA legibility (most readable), V4 Gallery desaturates everything so only Yves Klein blue pops (maximalist). Your choice depends on how you want to relate to Claude's brand identity. github.com/robertnowell/klein-blue

| Thu | dev.to | 2 | long | klein-blue, skill-tree |

| **Total** | | **24** | 8 long / 16 short | Each project: 6 posts/week |

Total character count short-form: ~8,400 chars (24 × average ~350)
Total word count long-form: ~2,500 words (8 posts × average ~310)

## Open calibration issues found while drafting

1. **konid post #5** (Mandarin examples) uses facts directly from the README example that aren't in `projects.yml` `facts[]` — the three Mandarin options and their English meanings. The validator should either (a) ground against README-level examples in addition to `facts[]`, or (b) facts[] should be expanded to cover example phrases the drafter might legitimately reference.
2. **klein-blue posts** reference specific hex colors (#d77757 Claude brand orange) that come from the klein-terminal-theme source code but aren't in `facts[]`. Same question — should facts[] include every hex the drafter might cite, or should the validator allow hex literals if they match source?
3. **klein-blue github URL** is aspirational — the actual repo at `~/klein-terminal-theme` is not pushed to GitHub yet. Either push it to `github.com/robertnowell/klein-blue` before the pipeline goes live, or the drafter should skip klein-blue until `repo` is a real URL.
4. **skill-tree post #8** (eleven-behaviors) references `9,830 conversations` — in facts[]. ✓
5. **skill-tree post #12** (growth-quest) uses a specific hypothetical quest example ("after Claude proposes an approach, ask it to argue against it") that isn't literally from the README. The validator should probably allow qualified hypotheticals ("For example, if your profile shows..." framing) as long as the claim structure is grounded.

These are all fixable during `validator.py` design in the next step.

## What passes the bar

All 24 posts pass on:
- No forbidden tokens (emoji, hashtags, exclamation, rhetorical question, `excited`, `thrilled`, `introducing`, `game-changer`, `AI-powered` etc.)
- No URL shorteners; full `github.com/...` paths
- Problem-first framing; AI is never the lead
- At least one specific number / technical choice / concrete problem per post
- Within channel length caps

## What this simulation tells us about the drafter

The hard parts of the drafter's job:
1. **Angle differentiation** — 4 angles per project × 4 projects × 4 channels = 64 distinct slot-outputs, and they must not be repetitive. Drafter needs strong angle-specific prompting.
2. **Length discipline** — short-form Bluesky posts routinely run 320–340 chars on first draft and have to be trimmed to ≤300. The drafter should generate at target length, not at "short enough then trim."
3. **Register transitions** — the same `facts[]` need to render as a 295-char Bluesky post, a 480-char Mastodon post, a 300-word Dev.to article, and a 340-word Hashnode article. That's four different density + framing problems on the same content.
4. **Code-block and scenario inclusion** — long-form posts need a real code block, screenshot, or concrete example. The drafter needs to know when to emit code and when not to.

None of these are blockers; they're the structural work the drafter prompt has to do. The voice rules alone are not enough.
